nativeRange.habitat: Quickly flowing rivers, slow streams, and stagnant pools across tropical Southeast Asia; rooted in soft, fine substrates in shaded backwaters and forest streams.
nativeRange.biotope: Southeast Asian tropical freshwater stream / blackwater pool

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich fine-gravel or aquasoil substrate with root tabs near the tuber
midground-to-background placement with vertical headroom for long lance-shaped leaves
soft to moderately hard, warm water in the mid-20s °C
moderate flow — avoid strong currents that tear the delicate leaves
stable parameters; avoid sudden swings that trigger melting

avoidWith: heavy snail populations (will graze tender new leaves)

summary: A striking Southeast Asian tuber plant prized for its long, lance-shaped leaves with rich red-to-purple undersides; a midground-to-background centrepiece that has been reported to undergo periodic dormancy cycles.
careNotes:
The Orchid Lily (Barclaya longifolia) is a tuber-forming aquatic plant native to tropical Southeast Asia, documented from Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, and Peninsular Malaysia. It belongs to the water lily family Nymphaeaceae, sharing that lineage with familiar Nymphaea water lilies. The tuber (the swollen underground storage structure from which roots and leaves emerge) should be only partially set into the substrate — bury the roots and the lower portion of the tuber, but leave the growing tip exposed; fully burying the tuber tends to cause it to rot.

The plant produces a rosette of long, narrow, lance-shaped leaves with wavy margins. Leaf coloration is often a striking red-to-purple-bronze on the underside and deep green to maroon-red on top, and tends to intensify under medium-to-high light with iron supplementation and CO2 addition. Water temperature is generally advised in the range of 22–28 °C, in soft to moderately hard water with a pH of 6.0–7.5, general hardness of 2–12 dGH, and carbonate hardness of 2–12 dKH. In well-maintained aquariums, the plant typically reaches 25–60 cm in overall height, with individual leaves measuring 12–30 cm; ultimate height varies with light intensity and tank depth. CO2 injection is not strictly required, but has been reported to produce larger, more colourful leaves and a denser rosette.

Barclaya longifolia has been reported to enter periodic dormancy, shedding most or all of its leaves before regrowing from the tuber — this is a normal part of the growth cycle, and the tuber should not be discarded during a dormancy period. A gentle substrate refresh or repositioning of the tuber has been suggested to encourage regrowth. The leaves are tender and easily grazed by snails and herbivorous fish, so tankmates should be selected with that in mind.
breedingNotes: Barclaya longifolia propagates in the aquarium primarily by separating daughter plants and side shoots from the parent tuber once they have developed their own roots. Mature plants can also flower: they produce both submerged, self-pollinating flowers (called cleistogamous flowers, which never fully open and pollinate themselves) and emergent, open-pollinating flowers (chasmogamous flowers, which open above the waterline and can be cross-pollinated). Seed propagation is possible through this route but is uncommon in the hobby; separating daughter plants and side shoots from an established tuber remains the most practical method for most aquarists.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied Tropica search URL; the search page itself did not surface a dedicated plantdetails page for Barclaya longifolia in the current Tropica catalogue, so cross-verification was carried out against Buce Plant, Flowgrow, aqua-fish.net, and aquariumlesson.com. The species is well-documented in the wider aquarium-plant trade under the canonical Nymphaeaceae assignment.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Nymphaeaceae), Southeast Asian origin, moderate-to-high light, pH 6.0–7.5, medium growth rate, midground-to-background placement, bulb/tuber planting technique, propagation via side shoots, and the deep-maroon-red coloration under high light + CO2 + fertilizer.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 18–30 °C (optimal 24–28 °C), pH 5.5–7.5, kH 2–20 dKH, CO2 10–40 mg/L, medium growth rate, background/midground placement (recommends ≥250 L tanks), and propagation via seeds / daughter plants / tuber splitting. Also notes that leaves are very attractive to snails.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 22–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–12 dGH, medium-to-bright light with 8–10 hour photoperiod, max height 30–80 cm with 30–50 cm individual leaves, midground-to-background placement, rhizome-division propagation, dormancy cycles, and the rule that only the roots — not the rhizome/tuber — should be buried.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Barclaya longifolia)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: confirms Nymphaeaceae / Nymphaeales, native range Indo-China to NW Peninsular Malaysia (with Myanmar and Thailand specifically documented), linear-lanceolate leaves 12–30 cm × 2–5 cm with red underside, and the dual cleistogamous/chasmogamous reproductive strategy.
